mnemonics.title: DVFS Memory Trick
mnemonics.content: Think of DVFS as 'Dial Voltage For Savings' - adjusting the dial (voltage/frequency) based on how much work your computer is doing to save energy.

mnemonics.title: C-States Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: C-States go from 'Active' (C0) to 'Completely Crashed' (C6) - deeper sleep = more energy saved but longer to wake up, like going from nap to coma.

mnemonics.title: PUE Formula
mnemonics.content: PUE = Total Facility / IT Equipment - Remember 'PUtting Energy' where IT equipment is the 'useful' part.

mnemonics.title: Race-to-IdLE
mnemonics.content: Race-to-Idle: Run really fast (RACE) so you can rest (IDLE) longer - like sprinting to finish work quickly to take a long break.

acronyms.usage: Runtime technique to adjust CPU power based on workload
Runtime approach to control power states of system components
Data center energy efficiency metric (Total/IT energy)
Inverse of PUE showing IT equipment efficiency percentage
Energy strategy of completing tasks quickly to reach idle state
Power states when CPU is not actively executing instructions
Active power states with different voltage/frequency combinations

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a spectrum: at one end MAX POWER + MAX PERFORMANCE (P0 state), at other end MIN POWER + MIN PERFORMANCE (Pn state). DVFS lets you slide along this spectrum based on workload.
Think of C-states as a staircase going DOWN: C0 = awake at top, C1 = light sleep, C3 = deep sleep, C6 = coma at bottom. Going down = more energy saved but harder to wake up.
Graph power (Y-axis) vs utilization (X-axis). Ideal: diagonal straight line (proportional). Reality: high power at low utilization (bowed curve). Goal: make the curve straighter.
Two pictures: Static = thermostat set once at install time. Runtime = smart thermostat adjusting to your actual presence and preferences in real-time.

analogies.analogy: Like driving a car - slow down (lower frequency) and reduce engine power (lower voltage) when going downhill or in traffic to save fuel, speed up when needed.
Like human sleep stages: awake (C0), light nap (C1), deep sleep (C3), coma (C6) - deeper sleep saves more energy but takes longer to wake up.
Like turning off the ignition in a parked car vs just idling - complete shutdown saves more fuel but needs restart.
Like cleaning your room quickly to then relax for the rest of the evening - finish fast so you can enjoy low-energy leisure time.
Like rotating seats in a row of people to share the warm spot near the fire - distribute the heat load evenly so no one gets too hot.
